learning_objectives: Recognize whether an exponential signal is growing or decaying from its sign.
Use the time constant to sketch decaying exponentials quickly.
Mark the key values 1, 1/e, and 1/e^2 on an exponential sketch.
Explain why every one-time-constant interval changes the signal by the same factor.

visual_plan.rationale: Use the textbook's Fig. B.10 because it is the canonical exam-facing sketch for time constants, then add an interactive exponential sketcher because students understand the role of a faster when they can change it and see the curve compress or stretch.
visual_plan.cram: Use visuals to memorize the sketch landmarks: start at 1, hit 0.37 after one time constant, hit 0.135 after two.
visual_plan.standard: Use the textbook figure plus one interactive demo to connect the formula T = 1/a with the visible curve shape.
visual_plan.top_score: Use visuals to notice that the 37% drop happens over any interval of length 1/a, not only from t = 0.

blocks.instruction: Render the first lesson page as a compact outline only. Use the heading 'Section Objective' followed by one sentence: 'Learn how to sketch exponential signals quickly using growth/decay direction and the time constant.' Then use the heading 'Concepts In This Section' and list only these concept names as bullets: 'monotonic exponential signals', 'decaying versus growing exponentials', 'time constant', 'quick sketch landmarks'. Do not add any expanded introduction, history, or background paragraph.

Start a new concept page with the heading '## 1. Recognize monotonic exponentials'. Explain in 90–130 words that a monotonic exponential keeps moving in one direction instead of oscillating. Contrast a decaying exponential with a growing exponential. State clearly that for a positive constant a, e^{-at} falls toward 0 as t increases, while e^{at} rises. Include one representative example: 'e^{-2t} decays faster than e^{-t} because the multiplier 2 makes the exponent become negative more quickly.' End with the exam note: 'The sign in the exponent tells you the direction; the size of a tells you how fast the change happens.'

blocks.explanation_instruction: Explain this as the standard decaying exponential. Define x(t) as the signal value, t as time, and a as the positive decay rate. Say to use it when the waveform starts high and moves toward 0. Exam trigger: a signal that loses a fixed fraction over equal time intervals. Common misuse: treating larger a as a taller starting value; it actually makes the decay faster.

Explain this as the standard growing exponential. Define a as the positive growth rate. Say to use it when the waveform increases by a constant factor over equal time intervals. Exam trigger: language such as 'grows exponentially' or 'increases by a factor e every fixed interval.' Common misuse: confusing e^{at} with e^{-at}; the missing minus sign changes the sketch direction.

mode_specific_visual_use.cram: Focus on the three sketch points: 1, 0.37, and 0.135.
mode_specific_visual_use.standard: Use the figure to connect time constant labels with the curve shape.
mode_specific_visual_use.top_score: Notice that changing a changes the horizontal spacing of the same percentage drops.

blocks.caption_instruction: One sentence: Fig. B.10 shows how a decaying exponential reaches 1/e after one time constant and 1/e^2 after two time constants.
blocks.description_instruction: Describe the two plots with horizontal axis t and vertical amplitude. Point out that e^{-at} starts at 1, falls to about 0.37 at t = 1/a, and falls to about 0.135 at t = 2/a. In the e^{-2t} example, emphasize that the same landmarks occur at t = 0.5 and t = 1 because the time constant is 0.5.

blocks.explanation_instruction: Start a new concept page with the heading '## 2. Use the time constant to sketch quickly'. Explain that T is the time constant of e^{-at}; it is the time needed for the signal to drop to 1/e, about 37% of its previous value. Define T, a, and 1/e. Include the representative example: for x(t)=e^{-2t}, a=2, so T=1/2=0.5; mark x(0)=1, x(0.5)=1/e\approx0.37, and x(1)=1/e^2\approx0.135. Common misuse: thinking the signal becomes zero after one time constant; it only becomes smaller.

mode_specific_visual_use.cram: Drag a to see how the sketch landmarks move, then memorize the landmark pattern.
mode_specific_visual_use.standard: Use the slider to connect a, T = 1/a, and the curve spacing.
mode_specific_visual_use.top_score: Test that every interval of length T gives the same factor drop, even if the interval does not start at zero.

blocks.instruction: Build a React + Canvas interactive demo on the same page as the time-constant explanation. Show the plot of x(t)=e^{-at}u(t) for t from 0 to 4. Include a slider for a from 0.25 to 4.0 with default a=2. Display T=1/a numerically. Draw vertical dashed markers at t=T and t=2T, and horizontal dashed markers at y=1/e and y=1/e^2. Label the points (T, 0.37) and (2T, 0.135). Include a toggle labeled 'show growing exponential e^{at}' that switches the curve and changes the note to 'growing exponentials increase by a factor e every T seconds.' Keep the visual clean: white background, navy curve, muted teal guide lines, muted red only for a short warning note. Add one observation prompt under the demo: 'When a increases, does the curve become wider or narrower?'

blocks.explanation_instruction: Start a new concept page with the heading '## 3. Why the same percentage drop repeats'. Explain in 100–140 words that the time constant is not just a first-interval trick. If two times are separated by T=1/a, the ratio of the later value to the earlier value is always 1/e. Define t1 and t2 as any two instants separated by one time constant. Use the quick example: if a curve has value 0.50 at some time, then one time constant later it is about 0.50/e, not 0.50 minus 0.37. End with the exam trap: time constants multiply by 0.37; they do not subtract 0.37.

blocks.instruction: Create a recap page titled '📌 Key Takeaways'. Include exactly 4 bullets, each no more than 22 words. The bullets must explicitly include these formulas: x(t)=e^{-at}, x(t)=e^{at}, T=1/a, and e^{-at_2}/e^{-at_1}=1/e when t_2-t_1=1/a. Mention the sketch landmarks 1, 0.37, and 0.135. End with one sentence: 'In the next section, we will use these sketching habits for more complicated signal shapes.'

questions.stem: For a positive constant a, which signal decays toward 0 as t increases?
questions.explanation: The negative sign in the exponent makes e^{-at} shrink as t increases.

wrong_option_explanations.A: e^{at} grows because the exponent becomes larger and positive.
wrong_option_explanations.C: a e^t grows with t; a only scales the height.
wrong_option_explanations.D: e^{-a} is a constant with respect to t, not a signal that decays over time.

questions.hint: Look for whether the exponent becomes more negative as t increases.

questions.stem: A student sketches e^{-4t} as a curve that starts at 4 and decays. What is wrong?
options: A. It should start at 1, not 4.
B. It should grow, not decay.
C. It should cross zero at t=4.
D. It should be negative for t>0.
questions.explanation: At t=0, e^{-4t}=e^0=1. The 4 changes the decay speed, not the starting value.

wrong_option_explanations.B: The negative exponent means it decays.
wrong_option_explanations.C: An exponential approaches zero but does not cross it.
wrong_option_explanations.D: e^{-4t} stays positive for all t.

questions.stem: For x(t)=e^{-2t}, what is the time constant?
questions.explanation: For e^{-at}, the time constant is T=1/a. Here a=2, so T=0.5.

wrong_option_explanations.A: 2 is the decay rate a, not the time constant.
wrong_option_explanations.B: 1 is the initial value e^0, not T.
wrong_option_explanations.D: 0.37 is approximately 1/e, the amplitude fraction after one time constant.

questions.stem: In a plot of e^{-at}, the curve reaches about 0.37 of its current value after what time interval?
questions.explanation: One time constant is 1/a seconds, and after that interval the value is multiplied by 1/e≈0.37.

wrong_option_explanations.A: a is the rate parameter, not the time interval.
wrong_option_explanations.C: e is the factor used in the amplitude ratio, not the time interval.
wrong_option_explanations.D: 1/e is the amplitude fraction, not the elapsed time.

questions.hint: Separate time interval from amplitude fraction.

knowledge_points.label: Repeated factor drop over equal time constants

questions.stem: If a decaying exponential has value 0.60 at some time, what is its value one time constant later?
questions.explanation: One time constant later, the value is multiplied by 1/e, regardless of where the interval starts.

wrong_option_explanations.B: The drop is multiplicative, not subtractive.
wrong_option_explanations.C: A decaying exponential approaches zero but does not become zero after one time constant.
wrong_option_explanations.D: Multiplying by e would describe growth, not decay.

questions.hint: A time constant changes the value by a factor, not by a fixed amount.

questions.stem: In the interactive sketcher, you increase a from 1 to 4 for x(t)=e^{-at}. What happens to the curve, and why?
questions.ideal_answer: The curve becomes narrower and decays faster because the time constant T=1/a becomes smaller. The 0.37 landmark occurs sooner.
grading_rubric: Must state that the curve decays faster or becomes horizontally compressed
Must connect this to T=1/a decreasing
Must mention that the 0.37 point occurs earlier
questions.explanation: This checks whether the student connects the parameter a to the visible sketch, not just to a memorized formula.
questions.hint: Ask what happens to T=1/a when a gets larger.

page-021.caption: Sketching (a) e^{-at}u(t) and (b) e^{-2t}u(t), illustrating the time constant and key amplitude values at 0.37 and 0.135.
page-021.description: The figure shows two coordinate plots with horizontal axis t and vertical amplitude, sketching the decaying exponentials e^{-at}u(t) and e^{-2t}u(t). In both plots the curve starts at value 1 at t=0 and decreases monotonically toward 0 for t>0, with labeled reference points marking 1/e ≈ 0.37 and 1/e^2 ≈ 0.135. The left plot labels these at t=1/a and t=2/a, while the right plot gives the specific case a=2 with the same amplitudes at t=0.5 and t=1. Students should notice the time constant relationship: after one time constant the exponential falls to about 37% of its initial value, and after two time constants to about 13.5%.
